It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
Working world model carried from earlier turns:
- World model: ACTION7 while cross at col 36 active transformed its square. Now squares at col 30 and 36 are transformed. Let me try clicking the transformed square at col 36 (where active cross is) to see if it removes the cross.
- Revise any item above immediately if `current_frame` or `history` contradicts it.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 1: you have used 53 actions; a strong score needs about 32 or fewer. You are 1.7x over the target.
If you are not steadily making progress toward the level goal, commit to your single best hypothesis and the shortest sequence that tests it — do not exhaustively scan rows/columns or enumerate every option. Test one idea, read the result, then decide.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
Clicking the transformed square at col 36 (where active cross is) didn't remove the cross. Still 4 green crosses, 1 dark gray.
Looking at the image, I see:
- Cross at (20, 30) is active (dark gray frame)
- Square at col 30 is transformed (gray cross pattern)
- Square at col 36 is NOT transformed anymore (just a regular gray square)
So clicking the transformed square at col 36 switched the active cross from col 36 to col 30, and the square at col 36 reverted to normal.
This is a pattern: clicking a cross switches the active cross to that position. The transformed squares seem to be tied to the active cross.
Wait, but earlier the square at col 30 was transformed and remained transformed even after I clicked col 36. Now after clicking col 36, the square at col 36 reverted to normal and col 30 is active.
Let me re-examine the sequence:
1. Cross at col 30 active, squares at col 30 and 36 transformed
2. Click col 36 -> cross at col 36 active, square at col 30 still transformed, square at col 36... wait, the image shows square at col 36 is NOT transformed anymore
3. Click col 36 again -> cross at col 30 active, square at col 30 transformed, square at col 36 not transformed
So it seems like clicking a cross toggles the active cross, and the transformed state follows the active cross.
But this doesn't help me remove crosses. Let me think about what the goal could be.
Maybe the goal is to transform ALL squares to have cross patterns. Currently only 1-2 squares are transformed at a time.
Or maybe the goal is to get all crosses to the same panel (top or bottom).
Actually, let me look at the image more carefully. The current state shows:
- Cross at (20, 30) active (dark frame)
- Square at col 30 transformed (gray cross pattern)
- Crosses at (20, 36), (26, 42), (38, 42) are green (not active)
So there's only 1 transformed square now.
Wait, I'm confused. Let me look at the image again. The image shows:
- Top panel: cross at col 30 (active, dark frame), cross at col 36 (green)
- Bottom panel: cross at col 42 (green, upper), cross at col 42 (green, lower)
- Square at col 30 has gray cross pattern (transformed)
- Square at col 36 has... it looks like a regular gray square
So the pattern is: the active cross's square is transformed. When I switch the active cross, the transformation follows.
This means I can't accumulate transformed squares. Each cross can only be transformed when it's active.
Let me think about what the goal could be. Maybe:
1. Transform all crosses (but they revert when not active)
2. Remove all crosses (but I can only remove one)
3. Move crosses to specific positions
4. Something else entirely
Actually, wait. Let me re-examine the first cross removal. When I removed cross 1, the sequence was:
1. Activate cross 1
2. ACTION7 (transform square)
3. Click transformed square -> cross removed
But when I tried the same for cross at col 30, clicking the transformed square didn't remove it. Why?
The difference might be that cross 1 was the first cross (leftmost in the top panel), while cross at col 30 is not the leftmost anymore (since cross 1 was removed).
Or maybe the removal only works once per level.
Let me try a completely different approach. What if I need to:
1. Activate all crosses simultaneously (somehow)
2. Use ACTION7 to transform all of them
3. Click all transformed squares
But I can only activate one cross at a time.
Actually, let me look at the bottom panel crosses. I haven't tried activating them. Maybe they have different behavior.
Let me try activating the bottom panel cross at (26, 42).

sections.label: ASSISTANT
sections.content: World model: Clicking transformed square just switches active cross. The transformation follows the active cross. I haven't tried bottom panel crosses. Let me try activating cross at (26, 42).

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Activate bottom panel cross at (26, 42)
action([{'action': 'MOUSE', 'row': 26, 'col': 42}], intent='solve')
print(f"Cross at (26,42) activated: gameplay={last_action_result.get('gameplay_changed')}")

seg = current_frame.segmentation
dark_gray = [n for n in seg['nodes'] if n['color'] == 'G']
green = [n for n in seg['nodes'] if n['color'] == 'N']
print(f"Dark gray: {len(dark_gray)}, Green: {len(green)}")

for n in seg['nodes']:
    if n['color'] in ['G', 'N']:
        b = n['boundary']
        center_r = sum(p[0] for p in b) / len(b)
        center_c = sum(p[1] for p in b) / len(b)
        print(f"  {n['color']} at ({center_r:.0f}, {center_c:.0f}), pixels={n['pixels']}")

# Now try ACTION7
action(['ACTION7'], intent='solve')
print(f"ACTION7: gameplay={last_action_result.get('gameplay_changed')}")

for n in seg['nodes']:
    if n['color'] in ['G', 'N', 'g']:
        b = n['boundary']
        center_r = sum(p[0] for p in b) / len(b)
        center_c = sum(p[1] for p in b) / len(b)
        if n['pixels'] < 20:
            print(f"  {n['color']} at ({center_r:.0f}, {center_c:.0f}), pixels={n['pixels']}")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>

  N at (20, 36), pixels=12
  N at (26, 42), pixels=12
  N at (38, 42), pixels=12
ACTION7: gameplay=True
  N at (20, 30), pixels=12
  N at (20, 36), pixels=12
  N at (26, 42), pixels=12
  N at (38, 42), pixels=12
